titleStmt.title: Letter from Marie Martin to her mother, Mary Martin, 18 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Marie Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Marie opens this letter to her mother by saying how little time she has had to write due to the fact that the hospital is so understaffed. She is clearly finding the work very hard and she refers to a patient of hers who had earlier died from dysentery and another who is very ill and she had written to his mother in Canada. She also tells her mother trivial things such as how most of the patients on the blue ward wear blue pyjamas. She takes a break from writing the letter and finishes on the 22nd November when she writes that her friend, Mr Boase, is returning to England and he will post the letter from there.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
18th November 1915 My Dearest Mother, It seems ages since I have
had time to write you a long letter
not since the day I wrote when
I had my days rest in bed, I
am glad to be able to say I
have felt A.1 ever since that day
& it is just as well as we have
been terrible busy infact one
day this week we were not
able to get off at all, a convoy
came in with very bad cases & we
were frightfully under staffed so there
was nothing for it, it was really
more satisfactory as it gave both
the patient & ourselves a better
chance & start as we were able to
get them all washed & comfortable
at the start, poor creatures what
they do suffer, one who came in on
Tuesday with Dysentery died this
morning & I have spent my time
off this afternoon writing to Canada
to a mans mother, he is very bad
but we are hoping with great care
all may go well, this is where
the sad part of our work lies
we are so short handed we are not
able to give the individual attention
necessary without neglecting the other
patients & instead of things getting
better they are getting worse as
so many orderlies & V A Ds are
getting knocked up, our staff is
2 shorter now as one has gone
ill & the others had to be taken
for another block as one of the six
that came out with me developed
S. Fever the other day but am glad
to say is getting on well & has been
sent to St Patricks Camp— Saturday 19th I had not time to
finish this the other day so am
continuing it to —day, I wish you
could see me & you would laugh
I am lying on my little bed
with hair hanging collar & Apron
of & enjoying the rest — very
much — We are in great delight
to—day as instead of having 140
beds to our Block it has been
divided & our Staff Sister is
only responsible for 7 Wards of
10 she has under her another
Trained Sister & myself & we have
five orderlies, so things are looking
a little brighter all though we
could do with more V.A.Ds, until
today I have had a terrible
week as all morning I spent in
Lower E & the afternoon Upper
We have some frightfully bad
patients with Dysentery we do
not seem to be getting so much
just now. Well I never
had time really to tell
you about my patients the
whole 71 are very nice but there
is one ward which we call the
blue ward they have mostly
Blue Pajamas which were sent
over from Ireland to Miss Bates
the other V.A.D who unfortunate
has got knocked up she is a
great admirer of E Carson
but an awfully nice girl
Well most of the Patients in this
ward are Gentlemen Tommies & they
are so nice & never grumble
about anything although at
times when we have been so
busy they have only seen us
about twice a day No 1 bed
is a great favorite of mine
a Mr Boasse so am asking
him to post this for me in
England when he goes. I am
sorry he is leaving us as
he was really very nice but
glad he is getting home for
Xmas. It was so funny to-day
something happened & his dinner
never came from the cook house
so we were all in distress to know
what to do so we gave him 2
lovely fresh eggs & asked one of
his pals to go up to Cook House
& have it cooked, it was just
my dinner time so had to go
off duty but ran down after
to see if he was alright before
I came off duty for afternoon
to find eggs still beside his
bed & could not get them
cooked anywhere down there
so had to bring them up
to my room & do them there —
which is 3 or 4 mins walk away —
however it was better than leaving him without
- I have another who is very bad &
asked me to write to his mother
which I did yesterday poor creature
he has been very ill for 4 weeks
& yesterday seemed very bad
indeed, he has lost so much blood
& they all said if they could
only get Burgundy or something
for him he might do, I was
off for a couple of hours last
night so went into Valetta
& bought some. I am glad
to say he is a little better
they say. I have not seen
him on account of being in
Upper E altogether today. It is
a terrible place for money every
thing is so scarce for patients
& when one realizes a little
extra thing may save a mans
life one never thingsbut I shall
have to close my heart unless
you can get up the little
collection I spoke of it is
the greatest charity possible
as otherwise it is impossible
for us ever to get them any
thing extra & things are
so short I think I shall
write to ask U Charlie of when
he is giving his Xmas charity
to loan me a £1 even if it
is only enough to buy them
chocolate or something for
Xmas to make a difference
for the poor creatures. Monday 22nd I had to stop this
letter the other day so am going to
finish it to-day with Luck & my
friend Mr Boase who is leaving
for England to-day has said he
will post it in England for me -
I am very sorry he is going as
he is awfully nice, if by any
chance he should be sent to
Ireland get the girls to look
him up & see that he has every
thing comfortable so that he will
go away with good impressions
of Ireland - Must end this
now as a message has just
come to say they are off in
a minute. I have my day
off to-day so will write a
serious letter to-day some time
Hoping this will find you
all very well
With heaps & heaps of Love Your most loving
daughter Marie
